title: Candidate overtone shear horizontal SAW resonators in thin-film lithium niobate for intermodal acousto-optic modulation
authors: Wenbing Jiang, Xuankai Xu, Yu Guo, Boyu Zhang, Lihui Jin, Xiao Chen, Tao Wu, Libing Zhou
abstract: The merits of thin-film surface acoustic wave (SAW) devices are pivotal to develop the high-performance intermodal acousto-optic modulators. In this work, we have proposed shear-horizontal (SH) SAW resonators for anticipated intermodal acousto-optic modulation on the thin-film lithium niobate platform. Through optimization of the cut angle of LN films, the SAW wavelength, and the thickness of interdigital transducer (IDT) electrodes, the calculated acousto-optic overlap factors utilizing SH0 modes are improved by more than an order of magnitude compared with those of Rayleigh modes. Furthermore, we have fabricated and characterized three kinds of proof-of-principle SH0 mode devices without/with grating reflectors. The electromechanical coupling coefficients (keff^2) and quality factors (Q) in the overtone resonators with grating reflectors are systematically evaluated, featuring the highest Q of 843 with the compromised keff^2 of 0.96%-4.72%. The results reveal that the temperature coefficients of frequency (TCF) of Rayleigh modes vary across various overtones, whereas the SH0 modes exhibit TCFs in the range of 32.3-68.9 ppm/C. Our fabricated SH0-mode overtone resonators demonstrate the capability of operating at power levels up to 29 dBm without electrode damage, offering a promising paradigm for robust and high-efficiency intermodal acousto-optic modulators with potential applications in integrated optical signal processing, microwave photonics,and quantum information technologies.
summary: This research proposes using overtone shear-horizontal SAW resonators on thin-film lithium niobate for advanced intermodal acousto-optic modulation. By optimizing device geometry, the authors significantly boost the coupling efficiency compared to conventional modes. The fabricated devices demonstrate robust operation at high power levels and exhibit superior temperature stability, making them promising components for integrated optical signal processing.
main_problem: The primary goal is developing high-performance intermodal acousto-optic modulators (AOMs) for integrated optical signal processing using thin-film lithium niobate.
main_result: The use of overtone shear-horizontal ($	ext{SH}0$) SAW resonators significantly improves the acousto-optic overlap factor and allows operation at high power levels ($29 	ext{ dBm}$) with superior temperature stability compared to Rayleigh modes.
method: The work combines theoretical modeling (calculating overlap factors, optimizing device parameters) with experimental fabrication and characterization using VNA measurements of admittance and resonance quality factors.
model_or_system: The system is based on SAW resonators fabricated on Y-cut thin-film lithium niobate ($	ext{LiNbO}_3$) platforms. The modulation relies on the interaction between an optical mode ($	ext{TE}0$ to $	ext{TE}1$) and the mechanical strain field generated by the Surface Acoustic Waves (SAWs).
key_observables: Electromechanical coupling coefficients ($k_{2	ext{eff}}^2$), Quality factors ($Q$), Acousto-optic overlap factor ($\Gamma_{ao}$), Temperature Coefficient of Frequency ($	ext{TCF}$), and operational power levels.
important_parameters: Optimal device parameters include the cut angle ($	heta=0^\circ$), SAW wavelength ($\lambda = 1.7 \mu m$), and IDT thickness ($h_{Al} = 150 	ext{ nm}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that $	ext{SH}0$ modes provide superior coupling compared to Rayleigh modes, and the high-order overtone resonances are utilized for enhanced performance.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate cross-sections of the resonators, schematic diagrams of the intermodal AOM process (TE0 to TE1 scattering), and plots showing measured admittance ($|Y|$), resonance quality factors ($Q_s$) vs. power/temperature, and TCF analysis for both $	ext{SH}0$ and Rayleigh modes.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from theoretical optimization of device parameters ($	heta$, $\lambda$, $h_{Al}$) to the physical realization and characterization of overtone resonators using grating reflectors (GRs). The final sections confirm high-power stability, superior TCF performance for $	ext{SH}0$ modes, and overall suitability for integrated AOM applications.
why_interesting: While the core physics is in acousto-optics/photonics, the focus on high-$Q$, stable resonators operating at defined frequencies (overtones) relates to cavity QED concepts. The ability to engineer mechanical resonances with high precision and stability could be analogous to engineering coupling strengths in quantum optical cavities.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. (a) Mock-up view of the SH0 mode resonator based on the lithium niobate-on-insulator substrate. (b) Schematic diagram of inter- modal AOM based on the SAW resonator. The input TE0 optical mode is modulated into the first-order optical mode TE1 under interactions of the actuated SAW phonon field with the resonance frequency Ω/2π and the propagation wavevector ka. The phase-matching condition of intermodal AOM is depicted in the right panel as well.

Fig. 1(a) depicts the cross-sectional view of the SH0 mode resonator in the LNOI platform. Aluminum (Al) is selected as the electrodes of interdigital transducers (IDTs) because of its low material damping and high electrical conductivity [7]. Fig.

Fig. 2. (a) Transformed photoelastic coefficients p11, p13, and p14 as a function of the cut angle θ with respect to the Y axis. The SAW propagation direction is along the X axis. (b) Simulated admittance curves for various cut angles about the Y crystal axis. The dashed lines traces the evolution trend of SH0 modes. (c) Extracted electromechani- cal coupling coefficients k2 eff versus the cut angle θ. (d) Simulated optical eigenmodes in the LN waveguide. The E-field profiles of TE2 mode is displayed at the waveguide width of 1.7µm.

Fig. 3. (a) Simulated admittance spectra with tuning the Al electrode thickness ranging from 100 to 450 nm at the increment of 50 nm. (b) Extracted k2 eff of the Rayleigh and SH0 modes, respectively. The displacement profiles of Rayleigh and SH0 modes are shown in the inset, respectively.
Fig. 5. Optical microscope images of the fabricated Device A (a), Device B (b), and Device C (c). The zoomed-in regions are the corre- sponding SEM images in each SAW resonator, respectively.
